content: If one root of the equation $${x^2} + (1 - 3i)x - 2(1 + i) = 0$$ is $$-$$1 + i, then the other root is
explanation:
$${x^2} + (1 - 3i)x - 2(1 + i) = 0$$

sum of roots $$ = - {{coefficient\,of\,x} \over {coefficient\,of\,{x^2}}} = {{ - (1 - 3i)} \over 1}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$( - 1 + i) + \alpha = - 1 + 3i$$ ($$\because$$ one root is $$ - 1 + i$$)

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 2i$$ $$\therefore$$ other root is 2i.

content: The equation $${x^2} - 3|x| + 2 = 0$$ has
explanation:
$${x^2} - 3|x| + 2 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow |x{|^2} - 3|x| - |x| + 2 = 0$$ ($$\because$$ $${x^2} = |x{|^2} = |x{|^2}$$)

$$ \Rightarrow |x|(|x| - 2) - 1(|x| - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow (|x| - 1)(|x| - 2) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow |x| - 1 = 0$$ or $$|x| - 2 = 0$$

If $$|x| - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow |x| = 1 \Rightarrow x = \pm 1$$

If $$|x| - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow |x| = 2 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2$$

content: If $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ be the roots of $${x^2} - a(x - 1) + b = 0$$, then the value of $${1 \over {{\alpha ^2} - a\alpha }} + {1 \over {{\beta ^2} - a\beta }} + {2 \over {a + b}}$$ is
explanation:
$${\alpha ^2} - a\alpha = {\beta ^2} - a\beta = - (a + b)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {{\alpha ^2} - a\alpha }} + {1 \over {{\beta ^2} - a\beta }} + {2 \over {a + b}} = 0$$

content: The sum of all real roots of the equation $$|x - 2{|^2} + |x - 2| - 2 = 0$$ is
explanation:
$$|x - 2{|^2} + |x - 2| - 2 = 0$$

Let $$|x - 2| = t$$

$$\therefore$$ $${t^2} + t - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow (t + 2)(t - 1) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ t = $$-$$2 or 1

If t = $$-$$2 then $$|x - 2| = - 2$$, but modulus of any value is non negative, so it has no solution.

If t = 1, then $$|x - 2| = 1 \Rightarrow x - 2 = \pm 1 \Rightarrow x = 3$$ or 1

$$\therefore$$ Sum of roots = 3 + 1 = 4

content: The quadratic equation whose roots are three times the roots of $$3a{x^2} + 3bx + c = 0$$ is
explanation:
$$3a{x^2} + 3bx + c = 0$$

Let roots of given equation are $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha + \beta = - {b \over a}$$

$$\alpha \beta = {c \over {3a}}$$

$$\therefore$$ New roots are $$\gamma$$, $$\delta$$ $$\therefore$$ $$\gamma$$ = 3$$\alpha$$, $$\delta$$ = 3$$\beta$$

$$\gamma + \delta = 3(\alpha + \beta ) = - {{3b} \over a}$$

$$\gamma \delta = 3\alpha \,.\,3\beta = {{3c} \over a}$$

$$\therefore$$ Quadratic equation is $${x^2} - (\gamma + \delta )x + \gamma \delta = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + {{3b} \over a}x + {{3c} \over a} = 0 \Rightarrow a{x^2} + 3bx + 3c = 0$$

content: If a, b, c are real, then both the roots of the equation $$(x - b)(x - c) + (x - c)(x - a) + (x - a)(x - b) = 0$$ are always
If $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ be the roots of the quadratic equation x2 + x + 1 = 0 then the equation whose roots are $$\alpha$$19, $$\beta$$7 is
explanation:
$$\because$$ $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ are roots of $${x^2} + x + 1 = 0$$ (given)

But $$\omega$$, $$\omega$$2 are roots of $${x^2} + x + 1 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha$$ = $$\omega$$, $$\beta$$ = $$\omega$$2 (say)

$$\therefore$$ $${\alpha ^{19}} + {\beta ^7} = {\omega ^{19}} + {\omega ^{14}} = \omega + {\omega ^2} = - 1$$ [$$\because$$ $${\omega ^3} = 1$$ & $$1 + \omega + {\omega ^2} = 0$$]

$${\alpha ^{19}}\,.\,{\beta ^7} = {\omega ^{19}}\,.\,{\omega ^{14}} = {\omega ^{33}} = 1$$

$$\therefore$$ Required equation is $${x^2} - ( - 1)x + 1 = 0$$

i.e. $${x^2} + x + 1 = 0$$

content: The roots of the quadratic equation $${x^2} - 2\sqrt 3 x - 22 = 0$$ are
explanation:
$$D = {( - 2\sqrt 3 )^2} - 4\,.\,1( - 22)$$

$$ = 12 + 88 = 100 > 0$$

$$\therefore$$ Roots are real & unequal

Moreover, coefficient of x is irrational.

Hence, roots are real, irrational and unequal.

content: The quadratic equation $${x^2} + 15|x| + 14 = 0$$ has
explanation:
$${x^2} + 15|x| + 14 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow |x{|^2} + 15|x| + 14 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (|x| + 14)(|x| + 1) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow |x| = - 14, - 1$$ (Both are impossible as $$|x| \ge 0$$)

$$\Rightarrow$$ No solution.

content: If sin $$\theta$$ and cos $$\theta$$ are the roots of equation ax2 $$-$$ bx + c = 0, then a, b and c satisfy the relation
explanation:
$$\sin \theta + \cos \theta = {b \over a}$$ & $$\sin \theta \cos \theta = {c \over a}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + 2\sin \theta \cos \theta = {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} \Rightarrow 1 + {{2c} \over a} = {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${a^2} - {b^2} + 2ac = 0$$

content: Let a, b, c be three real numbers such that a + 2b + 4c = 0. Then the equation ax2 + bx + c = 0

options.content: has its roots lying within $$-$$1 < x < 0
has one of the roots equal to $${1 \over 2}$$
has its roots lying within 2 < x < 6

explanation:
$$\because$$ a + 2b + 4c = 0

$$\therefore$$ $${a \over 4} + {b \over 2} + c = 0 \Rightarrow a{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^2} + b\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right) + c = 0$$

This shows that $$a{x^2} + bx + c = 0$$ has one of the root 1/2.

content: If the ratio of the roots of the equation $$p{x^2} + qx + r = 0$$ is $$a:b$$, then $${{ab} \over {{{(a + b)}^2}}}$$ =
explanation:
Let roots be am and bm.

$$\therefore$$ $$am + bm = - {q \over p}$$ & $$am\,.\,bm = {r \over p}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(a + b)^2}{m^2} = {{{q^2}} \over {{p^2}}}$$ & $$ab{m^2} = {r \over p}$$

On dividing we get $${{ab} \over {{{(a + b)}^2}}} = {r \over p}\,.\,{{{p^2}} \over {{q^2}}} = {{pr} \over {{q^2}}}$$.

content: If $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are the roots of the equation x2 + x + 1 = 0, then the equation whose roots ar $$\alpha$$19 and $$\beta$$7 is
explanation:
$$\because$$ $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ are the roots of x2 + x + 1 = 0 (given)

But, $$\omega$$ & $$\omega$$2 are roots of x2 + x + 1 = 0

Let $$\alpha$$ = $$\omega$$ & $$\beta$$ = $$\omega$$2

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha$$19 + $$\beta$$7 = $$\omega$$19 + $$\omega$$14 = $$\omega$$ + $$\omega$$2 = $$-$$1 [$$\because$$ $$\omega$$3 = 1]

And $$\alpha$$19 . $$\beta$$7 = $$\omega$$19 . $$\omega$$14 = $$\omega$$33 = 1

$$\therefore$$ equation having roots $$\alpha$$19, $$\beta$$7 is x2 $$-$$ ($$-$$1)x + 1 = 0 i.e., x2 + x + 1 = 0.

content: The quadratic equation 2x<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ (a<sup>3</sup> + 8a $$-$$ 1)x + a<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ 4a = 0 possesses roots of opposite sign. Then,
explanation:
To determine the range of values for $a$ such that the quadratic equation $2x^2 - (a^3 + 8a - 1)x + a^2 - 4a = 0$ possesses roots of opposite sign, we can use the property of roots in quadratic equations.

If a quadratic equation, $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$, has roots of opposite signs, then the product of the roots, which is given by $\frac{c}{a}$, must be less than zero. In this case, the equation is given by:

$$ 2x^2 - (a^3 + 8a - 1)x + a^2 - 4a = 0 $$

The product of the roots ($r_1$ and $r_2$) for this quadratic equation is determined by the constant term divided by the coefficient of $x^2$:

$$ r_1 \cdot r_2 = \frac{a^2 - 4a}{2} $$

For the roots to be of opposite signs, we must have:

$$ \frac{a^2 - 4a}{2} < 0 $$

Simplifying this inequality:

$$ a^2 - 4a < 0 $$

We can factorize the quadratic expression $a^2 - 4a$:

$$ a(a - 4) < 0 $$

This inequality holds when either $0 < a < 4$, where $a$ lies between 0 and 4. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: $0 < a < 4$

content: For what value of ' $a$ ', the sum of the squares of the roots of the equation $x^2-(a-2) x-a+1=0$ will have the least value?
explanation:
Let the roots of $x^2-(a-2)(x-a+1)=0$ be $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

$$ \therefore \quad \alpha+\beta=a-2 \text { and } \alpha \beta=-(a-1) $$

The sum of the squares of the roots is

$$ \alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2 \alpha \beta $$

Substituting the values

$$ \begin{aligned} \alpha^2+\beta^2 & =(a-2)^2-2(-(a-1)) \\ & =\left(a^2-4 a+4\right)+2 a-2 \\ & =a^2-2 a+2 \end{aligned} $$

To find the least value, we can rewrite this expression by completing the square

$$ a^2-2 a+2=(a-1)^2+1 $$

The minimum value occurs when

$$ (a-1)^2=0 \Rightarrow a-1=0 \text { so } a=1 $$

content: If the sum of the squares of the roots of the equation $x^2-(a-2) x-(a+1)=0$ is least for an appropriate value of the variable parameter $a$, then that value of ' $a$ ' will be
explanation:
Let the roots of the equation

$$ \begin{aligned} x^2-(a-2) x-(a+1) & =0 \text { be } \alpha \text { and } \beta . \\ \therefore \quad \alpha+\beta & =a-2 \\ \quad \alpha \beta & =-(a+1) \end{aligned} $$

The sum of the squares of the roots is $S=\alpha^2+\beta^2$

$$ S=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2 \alpha \beta $$

Substitute the expression in terms of ' $a$ '

$$ \begin{aligned} & S=(a-2)^2-2(-(a+1)) \\ & S=\left(a^2-4 a+4\right)+2 a+2 \\ & S=a^2-2 a+6 \end{aligned} $$

To find the minimum value, rewrite the quadratic.

$$ S=(a-1)^2+5 $$

The minimum value of $S$ occurs when $(a-1)^2=0$ which means $a=1$

content: If $$\mathrm{P}(x)=\mathrm{a} x^2+\mathrm{b} x+\mathrm{c}$$ and $$\mathrm{Q}(x)=-\mathrm{a} x^2+\mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c}$$ where $$\mathrm{ac} \neq 0$$, then $$\mathrm{P}(x) \cdot \mathrm{Q}(x)=0$$ has (a, b, c, d are real)

If $$a, b, c$$ are distinct odd natural numbers, then the number of rational roots of the equation $$a x^2+b x+c=0$$

content: If one root of $${x^2} + px - {q^2} = 0,p$$ and $$q$$ are real, be less than 2 and other be greater than 2, then
explanation:
Let $$p(x)=x^2+p x-q^2$$

Since, one of root $$p(x)$$ be less than 2 and other be greater than 2

$$\begin{array}{lrl} & \therefore & p(2)<0 \\ & \Rightarrow & (2)^2+p(2)-q^2<0 \\ & \Rightarrow & 4+2 p-q^2<0 \end{array}$$

content: If a, b are odd integers, then the roots of the equation $$2a{x^2} + (2a + b)x + b = 0$$, $$a \ne 0$$ are
explanation:
Discriminant = (D) = $$\sqrt{b^2-4ac}$$

$$(2a-b)^2 > 0$$, as 2 times of a is greater than b, then value of D is positive $$(D > 0)$$.

So, roots are rational.

content: The value of a for which the sum of the squares of the roots of the equation $${x^2} - (a - 2)x - a - 1 = 0$$ assumes the least value is
explanation:
Suppose $$\alpha,\beta$$ be the roots of the given equation

Then,

$$\alpha+\beta=a-2$$ and $$\alpha \beta=-(a+1)$$

$$\alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2\alpha \beta=(a-2)^2+2(a+1)$$

$$\alpha^2+\beta^2=a^2-2a+6=(a-1)^2+5$$

$$\alpha^2+\beta^2\ge5$$.

So, the minimum value of $$\alpha^2+\beta^2$$ is 5, which attains at $$a=1$$

content: Let $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ be the roots of the equation x<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ 6x $$-$$ 2 = 0 with $$\alpha$$ > $$\beta$$. If a<sub>n</sub> = $$\alpha$$<sup>n</sup> $$-$$ $$\beta$$<sup>n</sup> for n $$\ge$$ 1, then the value of $${{{a_{10}} - 2{a_8}} \over {2{a_9}}}$$ is
explanation:
Given, x2 $$-$$ 6x $$-$$ 2 = 0

$$\Rightarrow$$ xn $$-$$ 2(x2 $$-$$ 6x $$-$$ 2) = 0

$$\Rightarrow$$ xn $$-$$ 6xn $$-$$ 1 $$-$$ 2xn $$-$$ 2 = 0

When, n = 10,

Then, x10 $$-$$ 6x9 $$-$$ 2x8 = 0

$$\Rightarrow$$ x10 $$-$$ 2x8 = 6x9

Since, $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are roots of (i).

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha$$10 $$-$$ 2$$\alpha$$8 = 6$$\alpha$$9

and $$\beta$$10 $$-$$ 2$$\beta$$8 = 6$$\beta$$9

$$\therefore$$ ($$\alpha$$10 $$-$$ $$\beta$$10) $$-$$ 2 ($$\alpha$$8 $$-$$ $$\beta$$8) = 6($$\alpha$$9 $$-$$ $$\beta$$9)

$$\Rightarrow$$ a10 $$-$$ 2a8 = 6a9 ($$\because$$ an = $$\alpha$$n $$-$$ $$\beta$$n)

$$\Rightarrow$$ $${{{a_{10}} - 2{a_8}} \over {2{a_9}}} = 3$$

content: Let z<sub>1</sub> and z<sub>2</sub> be two imaginary roots of z<sup>2</sup> + pz + q = 0, where p and q are real. The points z<sub>1</sub>, z<sub>2</sub> and origin form an equilateral triangle if
explanation:
Given, z1 and z2 be two imaginary roots of z2 + pz + q = 0

$$ \therefore $$ z1 + z2 = $$ - $$p, z1z2 = q

z1, z2 and origin form an equilateral triangle.

$$ \therefore $$ $$z_1^2 + z_2^2 = {z_1}{z_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {({z_1} + {z_2})^2} - 2{z_1}{z_2} = {z_1}{z_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {p^2} - 2q = q \Rightarrow {p^2} = 3q$$

content: If P(x) = ax<sup>2</sup> + bx + c and Q(x) = $$ - $$ax<sup>2</sup> + dx + c, where ac $$ \ne $$ 0 [a, b, c, d are all real], then P(x).Q(x) = 0 has
explanation:
Given,

P(x) = ax2 + bx + c, Q(x) = $$ - $$ax2 + dx + c

Discriminant for P(x) = b2 $$ - $$ 4ac

and Discriminant for Q(x) = d2 + 4ac

If ac > 0, Q(x) has real roots and ac < 0, P(x) has real roots.

$$ \therefore $$P(x).Q(x) = 0 has at least two real roots.

content: Let a, b, c be real numbers such that a + b + c < 0 and the quadratic equation ax<sup>2</sup> + bx + c = 0 has imaginary roots. Then,

Again, f(x) has imaginary zeroes. So, a < 0.

Also, f(0) = c. Since f(x) is downward parabola. So, c < 0.

content: The greatest integer which divides $$(p + 1)(p + 2)(p + 3)...(p + q)$$ for all $$p \in N$$ and fixed $$q \in N$$ is
explanation: $$(p + 1)(p + 2)(p + 3)...(p + q)$$ is the product of q consecutive natural number $$(p,q \in N)$$. The product of q consecutive natural number is always divisible by q!.

content: If p, q are odd integers, then the roots of the equation $$2p{x^2} + (2p + q)x + q = 0$$ are
explanation:
Given equation,

$$2p{x^2} + (2p + q)x + q = 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$D = {(2p + q)^2} - 4(2p)(q)$$

$$ = 4{p^2} + {q^2} + 4pq - 8pq$$

$$ = 4{p^2} + {q^2} - 4pq$$

$$ = {(2p - q)^2}$$

= a perfect square

$$ \therefore $$ Given equation has rational roots

content: Let $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ be the roots of $${x^2} + x + 1 = 0$$. If n be a positive integer, then $$\alpha$$<sup>n</sup> + $$\beta$$<sup>n</sup> is
explanation:
We have, $${x^2} + x + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = {{ - 1 \pm \sqrt 3 i} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha = {{ - 1 + \sqrt 3 i} \over 2}$$ and $$\beta = {{ - 1 - \sqrt 3 i} \over 2}$$

or $$\alpha = {e^{{{i2\pi } \over 3}}}$$ and $$\beta = {e^{{{ - 2n\pi i} \over 3}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${\alpha ^n} + {\beta ^n} = {e^{{{2n\pi i} \over 3}}} + {e^{{{ - 2n\pi i} \over 3}}}$$

$$ = 2\left( {{{{e^{{{2n\pi i} \over 3}}} + {e^{{{ - 2n\pi i} \over 3}}}} \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ = 2\cos \left( {{{2n\pi } \over 3}} \right)$$

content: For real x, the greatest value of $${{{x^2} + 2x + 4} \over {2{x^2} + 4x + 9}}$$ is
explanation:
Let $$y = {{{x^2} + 2x + 4} \over {2{x^2} + 4x + 9}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{x^2}y + 4xy + 9y = {x^2} + 2x + 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow (2y - 1){x^2} + (4y - 2)x + 9y - 4 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = {{ - (4y - 2) \pm \sqrt {{{(4y - 2)}^2} - 4(2y - 1)(9y - 4)} } \over {2(2y - 1)}}$$

Since, x is real number.

$$ \therefore $$ $${(4y - 2)^2} - 4(2y - 1)(9y - 4) \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4{(2y - 1)^2} - 4(2y - 1)(9y - 4) \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4(2y - 1)(2y - 1 - 9y + 4) \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4(2y - 1)(3 - 7y) \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (2y - 1)(7y - 3) \le 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {3 \over 7} \le y \le {1 \over 2}$$



$$ \therefore $$ Maximum value of $$y = {1 \over 2}$$

content: If p, q are the roots of the equation x<sup>2</sup> + px + q = 0, then
explanation:
Given, p, q are roots of equation x2 + px + q = 0

$$ \therefore $$ p + q = $$-$$ p $$ \Rightarrow $$ 2p + q = 0 .... (i)

and pq = q

$$ \Rightarrow $$ p = 1

Put p = 1 in Eq. (i), we get

2 $$ \times $$ 1 + q = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ q + 2 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ q = $$-$$ 2

Hence, p = 1, q = $$-$$2

content: If $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are roots of ax<sup>2</sup> + bx + c = 0, then the equation whose roots are $$\alpha$$<sup>2</sup> and $$\beta$$<sup>2</sup>, is
explanation:
Given, $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are roots of ax2 + bx + c = 0

$$ \therefore $$ $$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$ = $${{ - b} \over a}$$

and $$\alpha$$$$\beta$$ = $${c \over a}$$

Now, if the roots are $$\alpha$$2 and $$\beta$$2, then

$$\alpha$$2 + $$\beta$$2 = ($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$)2 $$-$$ 2$$\alpha$$$$\beta$$

= $${\left( {{{ - b} \over a}} \right)^2} - {{2c} \over a} = {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{2c} \over a}$$ .... (ii)

And $${\alpha ^2}{\beta ^2} = {(\alpha \beta )^2} = {\left( {{c \over a}} \right)^2} = {{{c^2}} \over {{a^2}}}$$

The equation whose roots are $$\alpha$$2 and $$\beta$$2, is

$${x^2} - \left( {{{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{2c} \over a}} \right)x + {{{c^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${a^2}{x^2} - ({b^2} - 2ac)x + {c^2} = 0$$

content: Find the values of a for which the expression $${x^2} - (3a - 1)x + 2{a^2} + 2a - 11$$ is always positive.
explanation:
To determine the values of $ a $ for which the quadratic expression $ x^2 - (3a - 1)x + 2a^2 + 2a - 11 $ is always positive, we need to analyze the quadratic form and ensure that its discriminant is negative. This will ensure that the quadratic has no real roots and hence does not change sign, provided that the coefficient of $ x^2 $ is positive (which it is, being 1).

In general, a quadratic equation of the form $ Ax^2 + Bx + C $ is always positive if its discriminant ($ \Delta $) is less than zero. The discriminant for a quadratic equation $ Ax^2 + Bx + C $ is given by:

$$\Delta = B^2 - 4AC$$

For our quadratic expression $ x^2 - (3a - 1)x + 2a^2 + 2a - 11 $, we identify:

  • $ A = 1 $
  • $ B = -(3a - 1) $
  • $ C = 2a^2 + 2a - 11 $

Substituting $ A $, $ B $, and $ C $ into the discriminant formula, we get:

$$\Delta = (-(3a - 1))^2 - 4(1)(2a^2 + 2a - 11)$$

Simplifying $ \Delta $, we have:

$$\Delta = (3a - 1)^2 - 4(2a^2 + 2a - 11)$$

Expanding these terms:

$$\Delta = (3a - 1)(3a - 1) - 4(2a^2 + 2a - 11)$$

$$\Delta = 9a^2 - 6a + 1 - (8a^2 + 8a - 44)$$

$$\Delta = 9a^2 - 6a + 1 - 8a^2 - 8a + 44$$

$$\Delta = a^2 - 14a + 45$$

Now, we need to find when this discriminant is less than zero:

$$a^2 - 14a + 45 < 0$$

To solve this inequality, we first find the roots of the corresponding equation:

$$a^2 - 14a + 45 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula $ a = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} $ with $ a = 1 $, $ b = -14 $, and $ c = 45 $:

$$a = \frac{14 \pm \sqrt{196 - 180}}{2}$$

$$a = \frac{14 \pm \sqrt{16}}{2}$$

$$a = \frac{14 \pm 4}{2}$$

This gives us two roots:

  • $$a = \frac{18}{2} = 9$$
  • $$a = \frac{10}{2} = 5$$

Thus, the discriminant is less than zero for values of $ a $ that lie between these roots:

$$5 < a < 9$$

Therefore, the quadratic expression $ x^2 - (3a - 1)x + 2a^2 + 2a - 11 $ is always positive for:

$$5 < a < 9$$

content: Determine the sum of imaginary roots of the equation $$(2{x^2} + x - 1)(4{x^2} + 2x - 3) = 6$$
explanation:
Given equation is $$(2{x^2} + x - 1)(4{x^2} + 2x - 3) = 6$$

$$ \Rightarrow (t - 1)(2t - 3) = 6$$ [where $$t = 2{x^2} + x$$]

$$ \Rightarrow 2{t^2} - 5t - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow (t - 3)(2t + 1) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{x^2} + x - 3 = 0$$

or, $$2(2{x^2} + x) + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - 1)(2x + 3) = 0$$

or, $$4{x^2} + 2x + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = 1, - {3 \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Sum of imaginary roots $$ = - {2 \over 4} = - {1 \over 2}$$.

content: If the quadratic equation $$a x^2+b x+c=0(a>0)$$ has two roots $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ such that $$\alpha<-2$$ and $$\beta>2$$, then
If the equation $${x^2} - cx + d = 0$$ has roots equal to the fourth powers of the roots of $${x^2} + ax + b = 0$$, where $${a^2} > 4b$$, then the roots of $${x^2} - 4bx + 2{b^2} - c = 0$$ will be
explanation:
Let $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are the roots of $${x^2} + ax + b = 0$$ and the roots of $${x^2} - cx + d = 0$$ are $${\alpha ^4}$$ and $${\beta ^4}$$.

Now, $$\alpha + \beta = - a,\alpha \beta = b$$ ..... (i)

and $${\alpha ^4} + {\beta ^4} = c,{\alpha ^4}{\beta ^4} = d$$ ......(ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii),

$${b^4} = d$$ and $${\alpha ^4} + {\beta ^4} = c$$

$${({\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2})^2} - 2{(\alpha \beta )^2} = c$$

$$ \Rightarrow {[{(\alpha + \beta )^2} - 2\alpha \beta ]^2} - 2{(\alpha \beta )^2} = c$$

$$ \Rightarrow {({a^2} - 2b)^2} - 2{b^2} = c$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{b^2} + c = {({a^2} - 2b)^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{b^2} + c = {a^4} + 4{b^2} - 4{a^2}b$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{b^2} - c = 4{a^2}b - {a^4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{b^2} - c = {a^2}(4b - {a^2})$$

and for equation $${x^2} - 4bx + 2{b^2} - c = 0$$

$$D = {(4b)^2} - 4(1)(2{b^2} - c)$$

$$ = 16{b^2} - 8{b^2} + 4c = 8{b^2} + 4c$$

$$ = 4(2{b^2} + c) = 4{({a^2} - 2b)^2} > 0$$ = real root

Now, $$f(0) = 2{b^2} - c = {a^2}(4b - {a^2}) < 0$$ ($$ \because $$ $${a^2} > 4b$$)

= roots are opposite in sign.

content: If a, b$$ \in $$ {1, 2, 3} and the equation ax<sup>2</sup> + bx + 1 = 0 has real roots, then

options.content: number of possible ordered pairs (a, b) is 3

explanation:
We have,

ax2 + bx + 1 = 0

For real roots, D $$ \ge $$ 0

$$ \therefore $$ b2 $$-$$ 4a $$ \ge $$ 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ b2 $$ \ge $$ 4a

$$ \therefore $$ (a, b) = (1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 3)

$$ \therefore $$ Number of ordered pairs (a, b) = 3 and a is always less than b.

content: If the equation x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ 10x + 21 = 0 has real roots x = $$\alpha$$ and y = $$\beta$$, then
explanation:
Given,

x2 + y2 $$-$$ 10x + 21 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x2 $$-$$ 10x + y2 + 21 = 0

Now, for real roots of x, D $$ \ge $$ 0

100 $$-$$ 4 (y2 + 21) $$ \ge $$ 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ y2 $$ \le $$ 4 $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$-$$ 2 $$ \le $$ y $$ \le $$ 2

Also,

y2 = $$-$$ x2 + 10x $$-$$ 21

Now, for real roots of y,

$$-$$ 4(x2 $$-$$ 10x + 21) $$ \ge $$ 0

$$-$$ x2 + 10x $$-$$ 21 $$ \ge $$ 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ (x $$-$$ 7) (x $$-$$ 3) $$ \le $$ 0 $$ \therefore $$ 3 $$ \le $$ x $$ \le $$ 7
